name: Small Modular Reactors
summary:
Small Modular Reactors (SMRs) represent the most speculative yet highest-upside sub-narrative within the Nuclear Renaissance. Unlike traditional gigawatt-scale nuclear builds plagued by cost overruns and decade-long timelines, SMRs promise factory-manufactured, transportable units (typically 50-300 MW) with theoretically steep learning-curve cost reductions. The thesis hinges on a manufacturing economics paradigm shift: instead of bespoke mega-projects, SMRs aim to replicate the cost trajectory of aviation or semiconductors through standardized, assembly-line production. However, this remains largely unproven at commercial scale—NuScale's UAMPS project cancellation in late 2023 demonstrated that FOAK (first-of-a-kind) costs remain stubbornly high, and no SMR is currently commercially operating in the United States.

The narrative has been dramatically reinvigorated by the collision of two forces: AI-driven electricity demand growth that hyperscalers cannot meet with renewables alone, and a political realignment making nuclear energy bipartisan. Microsoft's Three Mile Island restart deal, Amazon's X-energy investment, and Google's Kairos partnership signal that the world's largest capital allocators are willing to fund nuclear development directly. The incoming Trump administration's 'nuclear bros'—including Energy Secretary nominee Chris Wright and pro-nuclear policy figures—promise regulatory acceleration, while the bipartisan ADVANCE Act (passed June 2024) already streamlines NRC licensing. The critical question for 2025-2030 is whether the gap between SMR renderings and operating reactors can be closed before impatient capital moves on.
key_drivers: AI/data center electricity demand: Hyperscalers (Microsoft, Amazon, Google) need 24/7 firm clean power at scale; renewables plus batteries cannot economically meet 100+ MW continuous loads for AI training clusters
Regulatory modernization: ADVANCE Act (2024) mandates NRC process reform, fee structure changes, and expedited licensing pathways specifically for advanced reactors

Political tailwind under Trump administration: Pro-nuclear 'nuclear bros' faction (Chris Wright, Doug Burgum) expected to accelerate DOE loan guarantees, streamline permitting, and prioritize domestic nuclear manufacturing

Corporate direct investment model: Tech giants shifting from PPAs to equity stakes and direct development partnerships (Amazon-X-energy $500M+, Google-Kairos), bypassing traditional utility procurement bottlenecks
Manufacturing cost-curve thesis: Standardized modular design enabling factory production, learning rates of 10-20% per doubling, and NOAK (nth-of-a-kind) cost targets 30-50% below FOAK

HALEU fuel supply bottleneck: Most advanced SMR designs require high-assay low-enriched uranium (5-20% U-235) not commercially produced in the US; Centrus Energy's Piketon facility and DOE investments are critical path
Grid resilience and energy security: SMRs offer distributed baseload, black-start capability, and reduced transmission build requirements—increasingly valued as grid reliability concerns mount
IRA and DOE subsidy stack: 45U nuclear production tax credit, 48E investment tax credit eligibility, and DOE Loan Programs Office backing create substantial de-risking for FOAK projects

adoption_curve.growth_rate: Projected 35-50% CAGR in installed capacity 2030-2040 from near-zero base; however, actual 2025-2028 growth is effectively flat as no commercial units will commission. Investment and order backlog growth is 60-80% annually.
adoption_curve.penetration_note: Essentially zero commercial SMR capacity operating in the US; globally only a handful of demonstration units (e.g., China's HTR-PM, Russia's Akademik Lomonosov floating plant). Represents <0.1% of global nuclear capacity.
inflection_points: First NRC-licensed SMR commercial operation in US (estimated 2029-2031: TerraPower Natrium in Wyoming, or Oklo's Aurora, or GE Hitachi BWRX-300 in Canada)—proves regulatory and technical viability simultaneously
HALEU domestic supply chain reaching 5+ tonnes/year production capacity (estimated 2027-2028), removing the single most critical fuel bottleneck for advanced reactor designs

First multi-unit order (3+ modules) from a single utility or corporate buyer demonstrating NOAK cost reduction of 20%+ versus FOAK—validates the manufacturing learning curve thesis that underpins the entire investment case
NRC licensing of 3+ distinct SMR designs by 2028, creating competitive dynamics and supply chain economies of scale across the vendor ecosystem
